behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real filesystem watch behavior across directory depth. A mock event emitter can hide bugs in recursive watch registration and path discovery. REAL-INFRA and MOCK-HIDE both point to System because the behavior depends on OS-delivered file events from nested paths. Rejected lower level using behavior text: "placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root" requires real watcher/subdirectory event propagation, not just local function logic.
behaviors.test_description: Run the daemon against a real temp input/output directory tree, create a nested subdirectory under the watched root, write a file into that subdirectory, and assert exactly one transformed output appears without restart.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: This behavior is primarily path+time-window decision logic and can be validated at one object/function boundary with a fake clock. MOCK-MISS does not require module handoff to reproduce the core failure. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: no real OS/network/subprocess, no AI dependency, no multi-step agent chain. Rejected higher levels using behavior text: "deduplication logic" and "same file path within a configurable time window" define deterministic local logic.
behaviors.test_description: Test the deduper component directly: inject a controllable clock, submit the same canonical path twice within the window (second rejected), then after window expiry (accepted). Include path normalization case if implemented (e.g., equivalent path forms).

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly names OS multi-event emission, which is a real infrastructure phenomenon. In-process fakes often emit idealized events and can miss duplicate-event patterns seen in real watchers. REAL-INFRA -> System, MOCK-HIDE -> yes. Rejected Integration using behavior text: "operating system emits multiple filesystem events" disqualifies purely in-process wiring tests.
behaviors.test_description: With a real watched directory and daemon process, perform a single file write pattern known to generate multiple events (create+modify/rename), capture processing count, and assert one pipeline execution/output for that file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: This is OS-level directory lifecycle behavior (delete/recreate inode/path changes) and watcher reattachment under real filesystem events. Mocks can miss watcher invalidation/re-subscription edge cases. REAL-INFRA and MOCK-HIDE require System. Rejected Integration via behavior text: "removed and then recreated while the daemon is running" requires real FS state transitions.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on a real temp watched directory, remove that directory while daemon stays alive, recreate it at the same path, add a new file, and assert it is processed without restarting daemon.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Ordering depends on arrival concurrency plus filesystem timestamp metadata under real watcher timing. A stub can test sorter logic, but real failures often occur at event/timestamp handoff and OS timestamp granularity. REAL-INFRA likely needed for true failure boundary. Rejected lower level using behavior text: "arrive at the same time" ties correctness to concurrent real event timing, not only pure sorting logic.
behaviors.test_description: Using real directories and running daemon, create several files in rapid parallel, record their creation timestamps as observed by the daemon/stat calls, and assert processing/output order follows timestamp order under concurrent arrival.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: This is a real filesystem write-completion race (event before write finish). In-process fakes typically do not reproduce partial-write visibility and close/flush timing accurately. REAL-INFRA and MOCK-HIDE both indicate System. Rejected Integration using behavior text: "still being written to when the arrival event fires" requires actual OS file I/O timing.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon with real input/output dirs, open a file and write in chunks with delays while keeping handle open, ensure daemon does not process during partial writes, then close file and assert processing starts only after completion.
